topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box arrived at precisely ten thirty, delivered by a courier whose uniform seemed unnervingly crisp. I stood by the door, my hand hovering over the handle, my gaze sweeping the darkened hallway. Inside, nestled in cellophane, were a dozen perfect white lilies. No note. Just the flowers. A prickle of unease traced a path down my spine. I carefully set the box on the entryway table, my fingers lingering near the ribbon. Every shadow in the apartment seemed to shift, and I found myself listening intently for any sound from the stairwell, the muffled thud of a closing door, anything that suggested an unwelcome presence. I didn't touch the wrapping paper; I just stared at the arrangement, waiting for the moment the silence would shatter.

[story 2]

When I opened the package, the scent of fresh gardenia filled the air, sharp and overwhelming. A bouquet of deep crimson roses lay on the polished wood of my desk. There was no accompanying script, no signature, nothing to explain the gesture. I picked up a single bloom, turning it over in my palm, feeling the cool, velvety texture. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I started pacing the room, my heels clicking sharply on the hardwood floor, checking the locks on every window, testing the deadbolt on the front door. My jaw tightened, and I kept my eyes darting toward the street outside, a low hum of apprehension vibrating beneath my skin. This felt too deliberate, too pointed.

[story 3]

The flowers materialized on my doorstep just as the afternoon sun began to dip, their vibrant yellow petals catching the light. I approached the threshold slowly, my movements measured and deliberate, like someone navigating a minefield. The arrangement was exquisite, clearly expensive, yet utterly devoid of personalization. I held the box as if it might be contaminated, my knuckles white where I gripped the cardboard. I didn't immediately open it. Instead, I circled the immediate vicinity, scanning the neighboring windows, my posture rigid, every muscle coiled tight. A faint tremor ran through my hands, and I could feel the temperature in the room dropping, despite the warmth of the late afternoon. I needed to know who sent this, and I needed to know *why*.
